channel.title: English on Usualtree
channel.description: Recent content in English on Usualtree

item.title: 雅思口语语料库 - Day 3
item.description:
雅思口语语料库 — C20 Test 3 Speaking（Museums / Satisfied with work / Job satisfaction）

一、Part 1: Museums

Q1: Did you enjoy going to museums when you were a child?

> “To be honest, I didn’t really go to museums on my own as a kid — mainly because I didn’t have the money or the freedom to travel around the city by myself. The only times I went were when my parents took me or when the school organized a trip. And I genuinely loved it. Seeing those real historical objects and dinosaur skeletons up close felt way more exciting than just reading about them in textbooks or seeing pictures on TV.”

Q2: Are there any interesting museums near where you live now?

> “Yeah, definitely. Shanghai has tons of interesting museums, but my favorite is the Shanghai Natural History Museum. What I love about it is that you can get really close to all these animal specimens and skeletons. Standing beneath those massive skeletons of prehistoric creatures, gazing up at them — it’s like you’re looking at traces of time itself. It really helps me disconnect from the daily chaos and just reflect on life and nature.”

Q3: Do you think it is best to go to museums by yourself or with friends?

> “Honestly, I prefer going solo. With friends, you’re constantly checking if they’re bored or rushing through stuff they don’t care about. Alone? I can wander around freely, spend forever staring at whatever catches my eye, and actually take it all in. It’s almost like meditation — just me and my thoughts.”

Q4: When you visit another city or country, do you think it’s important to go to a museum there?

> “Absolutely. I feel like museums are one of the best ways to really understand what a place is about — its history, its culture, all that. Plus, you can tell it’s a shared opinion because during holidays, everyone’s fighting to get tickets. They’re always sold out.”

二、Part 2: Describe a piece of work you felt very satisfied with

雅思口语语料库播放器（AI项目）

> “So, the piece of work I’m proud of is this IELTS speaking practice tool I made. It’s basically an app where you can paste in any text — like your own speaking notes or model answers — and AI will read it back to you in this really natural voice. > > I built it because I was prepping for IELTS myself and I got tired of just reading stuff off the page. I wanted to hear my own material spoken out loud, but obviously I couldn’t hire a voice actor. So I figured AI could do the job. > > AI did the heavy lifting on the voice side, but my friends were the real heroes here. I roped in like ten people who are also studying for IELTS, and they tested it, broke it, and gave me tons of suggestions. Their feedback was overwhelmingly positive, which was such a boost. > > What made me really satisfied wasn’t just that people liked it — it was that this was my first-ever product built with AI. That felt pretty huge, like I’d actually crossed some kind of personal threshold.”

三、Part 3: Being satisfied in life / Job satisfaction

Q1: What aspects of people’s lives can they often be dissatisfied with?

> “Man, where do I even start? It’s basically money and time, right? When you’re in school, you’re flat broke but you’ve got all the time in the world. Then you start working and it’s like — cool, I’ve got a bit of cash now, but when do I actually get to enjoy it? I’m always busy. There’s this song I really like, and there’s this line that goes like: ‘People are totally fine with cutting a few seconds off their lives just to buy another expensive watch.’ Like, we’re literally trading our time for stuff. It’s kind of messed up when you think about it. So yeah, my take is: stop worrying so much, just enjoy where you are right now.”

Q2: Is having ambitions in life always a positive thing?

> “Yeah, I’d say it’s definitely a positive thing. Having ambitions just brings so much passion and energy into your daily life. But I don’t think that means you need to be grinding away every waking moment. When you’re exhausted, you should absolutely let yourself rest. That’s the beauty of having ambitions — they act like a reset button. When you’re ready, they pull you back up and give you that positive drive to keep going.”

Q3: What are the most important components of a satisfying life?

> “Honestly, I’d say health is number one — without that, nothing else really matters. Then I’d say go easy on yourself; just let things flow and take life day by day. And having loved ones around you — family, close friends — that sense of being surrounded by love is huge. Oh, and having a bit of money in your pocket? Yeah, that doesn’t hurt either.”

Q4: What makes a job more satisfying: a high salary or having good colleagues?

> “Honestly, I’d say the job itself has to be something you actually care about — that’s number one. And then, solid colleagues and a good vibe at work matter a lot too. Because when you’re surrounded by people you click with, and you’re doing something you love, you just naturally bring your A-game. And at that point, the salary kind of takes care of itself.”

Q5: Do people need to change jobs regularly to stay satisfied at work?

> “Honestly, it really depends on what ‘satisfaction’ means to you. If you’re the type who’s always chasing a bigger paycheck, then sure, changing jobs regularly is probably the way to go. But if you value things like the work atmosphere and having a decent work-life balance, I don’t see the point in constantly looking for the next thing. I’d say find a place that feels right and just settle in.”

Q6: Is it possible to find job satisfaction in all types of work?

> “Honestly, I’d say yes. If you’re truly passionate about your work, you’ll naturally find meaning in it. It could be about contributing to society or simply satisfying your own interests. Either way, I believe every type of work has its own unique value.”

四、高分词汇 & 短语清单（可迁移）

  • tons of — 许多
  • get really close — 近距离接触
  • specimens / skeletons — 标本/骨骼
  • gazing up — 凝望
  • traces of time — 时间留下的痕迹
  • disconnect from — 从…中抽离
  • solo / flying solo — 一个人
  • wander around freely — 自由漫步
  • take it all in — 用心感受
  • meditation — 冥想（比喻独处）
  • one of the best ways — 最好的方法之一
  • fighting to get tickets — 抢票
  • sold out — 售罄
  • paste in any text — 粘贴任意文本
  • read it back — 读回
  • natural voice — 自然语音
  • prepping for — 准备（口语）
  • tired of — 厌倦
  • heavy lifting — 繁重工作
  • roped in — 拉入；招募
  • tested it, broke it — 测试并找出问题
  • overwhelmingly positive — overwhelmingly 正面
  • first-ever product — 第一个产品
  • crossed some kind of personal threshold — 跨越个人里程碑
  • flat broke — 彻底没钱
  • all the time in the world — 大把时间
  • a bit of cash — 有点积蓄
  • trading our time for stuff — 用时间换物质
  • stop worrying so much — 别想太多
  • enjoy where you are right now — 享受当下
  • brings so much passion and energy — 带来激情和活力
  • grinding away — 拼命奋斗
  • reset button — 重启键
  • pull you back up — 让你重新振作
  • health is number one — 健康第一
  • go easy on yourself — 别对自己太苛刻
  • let things flow — 顺其自然
  • loved ones around you — 身边有爱的人
  • doesn’t hurt either — 也不错/锦上添花
  • something you actually care about — 你真正在乎的事
  • solid colleagues — 靠谱的同事
  • good vibe — 好氛围
  • click with — 合得来
  • bring your A-game — 全力以赴
  • salary takes care of itself — 薪资自然跟上
  • always chasing a bigger paycheck — 一直追求更高薪水
  • changing jobs regularly — 经常换工作
  • work atmosphere — 工作氛围
  • decent work-life balance — 不错的工作生活平衡
  • constantly looking for the next thing — 骑驴找马
  • find a place that feels right — 找到合适的地方
  • settle in — 安定下来
  • truly passionate — 真正热爱
  • naturally find meaning — 自然找到意义
  • contributing to society — 贡献社会
  • satisfying your own interests — 满足个人兴趣
  • own unique value — 独特价值

item.title: 雅思口语语料库 - Day 4
item.description:
雅思口语语料库 — C20 Test 4 Speaking（Personal Qualities / News / Discussion）

一、Part 1: Personal Qualities

Q1: What do you think your best personal qualities are?

> “Honestly, I’d say it’s self-reflection. I truly believe that looking inward and asking yourself what you could’ve done better is the only way to actually level up as a person. I do this pretty regularly — after a presentation, after a tough week — and I think it’s the main reason I’ve been able to keep progressing.”

Q2: Do you have the same personal qualities as your parents?

> “Honestly, I’d say we’re all pretty meticulous about things. My parents drilled into me from a young age that you need to pay attention to the details — doesn’t matter if it’s household stuff or study problems. And they didn’t just talk about it; they actually practiced what they preached. I grew up watching them handle everything carefully, so that habit just rubbed off on me.”

Q3: What personal qualities are important to you in a friend?

> “I’d say sincerity and kindness are the most important. Everyone has flaws, and you’re bound to have disagreements with friends. But as long as I feel their core nature is genuinely good and honest, I can look past the smaller issues. Those don’t really matter in the long run.”

Q4: Do you think you have the personal qualities to be a good/successful leader?

> “To be honest, I don’t think so — not yet, anyway. I feel like I’m still missing some key qualities. But it’s definitely something I aspire to, so I’m constantly trying to learn and improve myself.”

二、Part 2: Describe a time when you had a long discussion about a news story

IG夺冠（英雄联盟全球总决赛）

> “Well, I’d like to talk about the time when IG won the League of Legends World Championship. I was in high school at the time, and my classmates and I talked about it for days. > > Basically, the moment they won, every guy in my class came in the next day asking, ‘Did you watch the game? IG is insane!’ For all of us who played LOL, it was huge because it was the first time a Chinese team had won the world title. We’d been following the tournament for years and had faced so many disappointments before, so this finally felt like the world was hearing our story. > > Even our teacher brought it up in class, which was pretty rare. We just couldn’t stop talking about it because it was this historic moment that we’d all been waiting for for so long.”

三、Part 3: News / Discussion programmes

Q1: How do most people find out about the news in your country?

> “I would contend that digital platforms, particularly mobile devices, have become the predominant conduit for news consumption across all demographics, encompassing both the elderly and the younger generation. The ubiquity of smartphones has effectively supplanted traditional print and broadcast media as the primary source of information.”

Q2: Are people more interested in local news than national news?

> “Honestly, I’d say people care about both equally. Local news is obviously relevant because it’s about what’s happening right around you — your neighbourhood, your city, your commute. But national news isn’t something people tune out either, because things like economic policy or education reform directly shape your future too. Most people I know follow both — they just consume them differently.”

Q3: How important is it to know about international news?

> “Well, for the average person, I’d say it’s not absolutely essential. But if you’re working in an international field — like business, tech, or academia — or if you aspire to contribute on a global scale, then keeping up with international news is pretty important. It really depends on your personal and professional goals.”

Q4: Why are discussion programmes involving members of the public popular on TV and radio?

> “Honestly, I think it’s because they feel real. When a random person calls in or sits on a panel and talks about their actual life problems or opinions, the audience is like, ‘That’s exactly how I feel.’ It creates this instant connection. Plus, there’s a sense that anyone’s voice matters, which makes people feel invested in the show.”

Q5: What kinds of people want to take part in discussion programmes?

> “Well, I’d say there are a few types. Some people are just naturally opinionated — they love sharing their views on public platforms. Others might have gone through specific experiences — like consumer disputes or neighbourhood conflicts — and they want to get their story heard or seek advice. And then there are people who simply enjoy being on camera or crave a bit of attention.”

Q6: Do discussion programmes influence people in a good or bad way?

> “I would argue that the influence is ambivalent and context-dependent. Constructive programmes can elevate public discourse, foster empathy, and broaden perspectives by exposing audiences to diverse lived experiences. Conversely, programmes that prioritise sensationalism — deliberately amplifying discord or selecting participants for maximal controversy — may exacerbate social polarisation and degrade the quality of public debate. The net effect is therefore determined by editorial intent.”

四、高分词汇 & 短语清单（可迁移）

  • self-reflection / looking inward — 自省
  • level up — 进步提升
  • drilled into me — 被反复教导
  • practiced what they preached — 言传身教
  • rubbed off on me — 耳濡目染
  • core nature — 底色/本质
  • look past — 不计较/忽略
  • not yet, anyway — 目前还没有
  • aspire to — 渴望成为
  • constantly trying to learn and improve — 不断学习成长
  • first time a Chinese team had won — 中国首次夺冠
  • following the tournament for years — 多年关注赛事
  • so many disappointments — 多次失望
  • hearing our story — 让世界听到我们的故事
  • historic moment — 历史性时刻
  • predominant conduit — 主要渠道
  • supplanted — 取代
  • across all demographics — 跨所有人群
  • tune out — 忽视
  • directly shape — 直接塑造
  • not absolutely essential — 并非绝对必要
  • global scale — 全球层面
  • instant connection — 即时共鸣
  • anyone’s voice matters — 每个人的声音都重要
  • naturally opinionated — 天生爱表达观点
  • get their story heard — 让故事被听到
  • crave a bit of attention — 渴望一点关注
  • ambivalent and context-dependent — 矛盾且取决于情境
  • elevate public discourse — 提升公共讨论
  • foster empathy — 培养共情
  • prioritise sensationalism — 优先追求轰动效应
  • amplifying discord — 放大分歧
  • exacerbate social polarisation — 加剧社会两极分化
  • determined by editorial intent — 取决于编辑意图

item.title: 雅思口语语料库 - Day 2
item.description:
雅思口语语料库 — C20 Test 2 Speaking（Fruit / Changed a plan / Career）

一、Part 1: Fruit

Q1: What’s your favourite fruit?

> “Definitely mango. I just love the taste — it’s sweet but not overwhelming. Plus, I have this childhood memory of a mango tree right outside my house. Every summer it would produce these huge mangoes, and my mum would pick them for me. I get really nostalgic thinking about those days.”

Q2: Are there any kinds of fruit that you don’t like eating?

> “Honestly, I don’t really dislike any fruit. But if I had to pick one, I’d probably say apples. I mean, they’re super healthy, but they’re just so common that they feel a bit boring. Compared to mangoes or peaches, they’re not very juicy, and the flavor is a bit bland. Maybe that’s just my bias though.”

Q3: Do you like eating cooked food that has fruit in it?

> “Not really. I just find it off-putting — both the look and the taste. There’s something weird about hot, soggy fruit in a savoury dish; it gets all mushy and overly sweet. The only exception I’d make is pineapple — like in sweet-and-sour pork or on pizza. For some reason, that works for me.”

Q4: Where’s the best place to buy fruit where you live?

> “Honestly, the fruit shop on campus is kind of overpriced, so I usually avoid it. Instead, I buy from these small stalls right outside the school gate — they’re way cheaper. Or I go to Hema, which is like a fresh grocery store here. The quality there is definitely better, but the street stalls are unbeatable when it comes to price.”

二、Part 2: Describe a time when you changed a plan

原计划去银川，改去福州参加女友毕业典礼

> “Well, I’d like to talk about a time just after I passed my postgraduate interview in Shanghai. My original plan was to fly straight to Yinchuan to hang out with some friends and unwind. But then I realized my girlfriend’s graduation ceremony was coming up really soon, and I was worried I might miss it if I went to Yinchuan first. So I changed my plan completely — I cancelled the trip to see my friends and headed straight back to Fuzhou instead. > > I felt genuinely happy and relieved that I made it to her ceremony. It meant a lot to be there for such an important milestone in her life, and I was also thrilled to share the news of getting accepted with her in person right away.”

三、Part 3: Making plans with friends / Planning a career

Q1: What kinds of plans do friends make together?

> “I’d say travel plans are probably the most common ones. Like, my friends and I are always scheming short weekend trips — just hopping on a train to nearby cities, hitting up local restaurants, and checking out whatever sights are there. Sometimes it’s just a day trip to a nearby town for good food and a change of scenery.”

Q2: Do you think it’s better to discuss future plans with friends or with family?

> “I would argue that neither is superior; rather, they fulfil distinct functions. Family discussions tend to provide emotional scaffolding — unconditional support and encouragement — even if the advice lacks professional specificity. Conversely, peers can offer multifaceted perspectives rooted in shared generational experiences, and their support tends to be both empathetic and pragmatically effective because they face analogous challenges.”

Q3: When making plans for the future, is it important not to copy friends?

> “Yeah, absolutely. I don’t think we should blindly copy our friends’ paths. Everyone’s situation is different. We should really listen to our own instincts and stay committed to our personal goals, making plans based on our unique strengths and circumstances.”

Q4: When people are choosing what to study, how important is it that their course should lead directly to a career?

> “Well, I think it’s pretty important, especially nowadays. University education in China often lags behind the industry — it’s either too theory-heavy or purely research-oriented. For students who want to land a job straight after graduation, that’s a real disadvantage. If you want to map out a solid career path, you pretty much have to pick up practical, industry-relevant skills on your own. That said, the foundational knowledge and general education you get at university are still crucial — they’re basically the bedrock of whatever career you pursue.”

Q5: Why is it a good idea to get some work experience before deciding on a future career?

> “Well, like I just said, you need to get your hands dirty with industry stuff. An internship lets you dive straight into how a company actually works, so you pick up practical skills way faster and more accurately than in a classroom. There’s this Chinese saying — basically, book knowledge only gets you so far; to truly understand something, you gotta do it yourself. Work experience is exactly that.”

Q6: How easy do you think it is for people to change from one career to another?

> “I think it really depends on the role. If you’re aiming for something technical that requires specialized knowledge — like engineering or programming — then switching fields takes serious effort. But if it’s more repetitive work or service-oriented jobs, it’s probably much easier. It also depends on what you were doing before. Generally speaking, people from more demanding roles can transition to simpler ones more smoothly — it’s almost like natural downward compatibility.”

四、高分词汇 & 短语清单（可迁移）

  • nostalgic — 怀念的
  • off-putting — 令人反感的
  • mushy and overly sweet — 甜腻软烂的
  • overpriced / unbeatable — 太贵/无可比拟（价格）
  • unwind / decompress — 放松
  • coming up really soon — 即将开始
  • made it — 赶上
  • meant a lot — 意义重大
  • in person — 面对面
  • scheming — 策划（口语）
  • hopping on a train — 坐火车
  • hitting up — 去（某地）
  • emotional scaffolding — 情感支撑
  • professional specificity — 专业针对性
  • multifaceted perspectives — 多层面视角
  • analogous challenges — 相似挑战
  • blindly copy — 盲目模仿
  • listen to your own instincts — 听从内心
  • lags behind — 落后
  • theory-heavy / research-oriented — 偏重理论/研究导向
  • land a job — 找到工作
  • map out — 规划
  • pick up on your own — 自学
  • bedrock — 基石
  • get your hands dirty — 亲自动手实践
  • book knowledge only gets you so far — 纸上得来终觉浅（地道表达）
  • do it yourself — 绝知此事要躬行（亲自去做）
  • specialized knowledge — 专业知识
  • takes serious effort — 需要付出很大努力
  • service-oriented — 服务导向的
  • demanding roles — 要求高/难度大的岗位
  • natural downward compatibility — 自然的向下兼容

item.title: 雅思口语语料库
item.description:
雅思口语语料库 — C20 Test 1

一、自我介绍（ID Check）

姓名

> “Hi, I’m Liu Changshu, but Tree works too — T-R-E-E. It’s because ‘shu’ in Chinese basically sounds like ’tree’.”

来自哪里

> “I’m from Fuzhou, a coastal city in southeastern China. It’s actually the capital of Fujian province, famous for its tea culture and seafood.”

学校专业

> “I’m a grad student at ShanghaiTech, studying EE — that’s Electrical Engineering. It’s a pretty small but research-heavy university here in Shanghai.”

二、Part 1: Walking

Q1: How much walking do you do in your daily life?

> “To be honest, I don’t walk much on weekdays because I usually ride an e-scooter to my lab, and since the campus is pretty small, there aren’t many chances to walk around. But on weekends, I love doing city walks in downtown Shanghai — places like Wukang Road or Huaihai Road. Shanghai is just such a comfortable city to explore on foot.”

Q2: Did you walk more when you were at school than now?

> “Yeah, I definitely walked more back then. I’m still a student now, but during my undergrad at Fuzhou University, the campus was huge and I lived on the sixth floor — and there was no elevator at all. So I had to climb up and down the stairs every single day. These days, my dorm has an elevator and I ride an e-scooter everywhere, so my walking has really dropped.”

Q3: What places are there to go for a walk near where you live?

> “Like I mentioned, I don’t really walk around campus much. I usually take the subway to the Bund or Lujiazui. I just love the vibe there — the mix of old and new architecture — and I actually enjoy how it’s always bustling with tourists. It feels really lively.”

Q4: Would you ever like to go on a walking holiday?

> “I’d love to, honestly. Right now my research and coursework are pretty intense, so it’s hard to find a big chunk of time. But I firmly believe that you should do the things you really want while you’re still young. So I’m thinking of squeezing in a short trip — maybe just a weekend or during a long break — rather than putting it off forever.”

三、Part 2: Describe a play or a film you’d like to see again with friends

Film: Avengers: Endgame

> “Well, the film I’d love to see again is Avengers: Endgame. I first watched it back in high school with a bunch of my classmates, and honestly, it was such a big deal for all of us at the time. > > To be honest, I’m not usually the kind of person who rewatches superhero movies, but this one is special because it basically opened the door to Marvel films for me. Before that, I hadn’t really been into Western superhero stuff, but Endgame completely changed my mind. > > I still remember my friends were absolutely obsessed with the characters — Iron Man, Captain America, all of them. They kept saying how exhilarating and action-packed it was, and that the final battle was just insanely satisfying. For them, it wasn’t just a movie; it was like the ultimate payoff for years of storytelling. > > But for me, the reason I want to see it again isn’t just about the film itself. It’s more about the feeling I had back then. I was young, carefree, and sitting in that cinema with my closest friends made me feel like the world was full of possibilities, you know? Everything felt larger-than-life, and I genuinely believed my future could be just as epic as the plot. > > So if I had the chance, I’d definitely go with those same high school friends. I guess part of me just wants to relive that moment and maybe recapture a bit of that optimism and excitement. Plus, now that we’re all busy with grad school and work, it’d be nice to just hang out like we used to, without worrying about deadlines or anything.”

四、Part 3: Theatres today / Actors

Q1: What are the most popular kinds of plays or shows at theatres in your country?

> “Well, I don’t think China has a particularly strong theatre culture, at least not in the way some European countries do. Traditional plays do exist, but they tend to attract a relatively small audience. In terms of what’s genuinely popular right now, I’d say stand-up comedy, live music, and e-sports tournaments are the dominant forms of live entertainment, especially among young people.”

Q2: How easy is it to get tickets to the theatre?

> “It really depends on how popular the show is. Buying tickets on your phone is super convenient these days — it’s just a few taps. But if it’s a really hot show, it can sell out in literally seconds.”

Q3: Do you think theatres need to do more to attract younger audiences?

> “Yeah, I think so. The thing is, traditional theatre performances often feel a bit disconnected from young people’s daily lives and culture. Since young people are basically the main consumer force now, theatres really need to adapt — whether it’s for keeping traditions alive or just staying in business. They should probably update their content or format to match what younger audiences actually enjoy.”

Q4: What do you think attracts people to working as an actor?

> “Well, I guess the money and fame definitely play a big part. But personally, I think what really draws people in is the chance to step into someone else’s shoes — to use your own emotions and understanding of life to bring a character to life. It’s a way to express your perspective to the audience, and at the same time, you get to experience a completely different life, which is pretty amazing. I think for creative people, expressing yourself is almost like a calling, and exploring different human experiences is what keeps them going.”

Q5: What qualities does a person need to become an actor? Any disadvantages?

> “Well, first of all, you definitely need to be resilient and willing to put in the hard work. Looks matter, but they’re not everything. What’s more important is having your own perspective on life — your own attitude and understanding of the world. Plus, you need to genuinely love performing and be able to convey emotions effectively. > > As for the downsides, I think actors sacrifice a huge chunk of their personal life. Their strengths and weaknesses are constantly under a microscope, and they can easily get swamped by public opinion — whether it’s praise or criticism.”

五、高分词汇 & 短语清单（可迁移到其他话题）

  • e-scooter — 电动车
  • city walks / explore on foot — 城市漫步
  • the vibe — 氛围
  • bustling with tourists — 熙熙攘攘
  • undergrad — 本科时期
  • climb up and down — 爬上爬下
  • squeezing in — 挤出时间
  • putting it off forever — 一直拖延
  • opened the door to… — 打开了…的大门
  • absolutely obsessed with — 对…超级痴迷
  • exhilarating — 令人兴奋的
  • action-packed — 动作场面丰富的
  • insanely satisfying — 极其过瘾的
  • carefree — 无忧无虑的
  • larger-than-life — 史诗般的
  • relive that moment — 重温那一刻
  • recapture — 重新找回
  • hang out like we used to — 像过去那样一起混
  • strong theatre culture — 浓厚的戏剧文化
  • relatively small audience — 相对小众的受众
  • dominant forms — 主流形式
  • sell out in literally seconds — 一秒售罄
  • disconnected from — 与…脱节
  • main consumer force — 消费主流
  • keeping traditions alive — 传承
  • staying in business — 维持经营
  • draws people in — 吸引人们
  • step into someone else’s shoes — 代入他人角色
  • bring a character to life — 赋予角色生命
  • express your perspective — 表达观点
  • like a calling — 像一种使命
  • resilient — 坚韧不拔的
  • put in the hard work — 吃苦耐劳
  • convey emotions — 传达情绪
  • sacrifice a huge chunk of… — 牺牲很大一部分…
  • under a microscope — 被放大审视
  • swamped by public opinion — 淹没在舆论中
